title: Eggs: Selection, Preparation, and Cooking
content:
SELECTION

Choose shells with dull bloom (not shiny, which indicates age). Color of shell (white or brown) has no relation to interior quality. Heavy egg = fresh; lightness indicates liquefaction. Fresh egg will not rattle when shaken. Grade A, dated, "large" size (2 ounces) standard for recipes.

SIZE EQUIVALENTS
1 large egg = 2 tablespoons white + 1 tablespoon + 1 teaspoon yolk
2 large eggs = ½ cup
3 medium eggs = ½ cup

SEPARATING

Tap center of side sharply on bowl edge. Pull edges apart with thumbs, allowing white to flow into small bowl while yolk remains in shell half. Pour yolk back and forth between halves until all white is removed. Transfer yolk to separate bowl. Even tiny amount of yolk in whites reduces whipping ability.

BEATING

Whole eggs/yolks: Beat vigorously until light in color and texture. Electric mixer: up to 5 minutes or more, increases up to 6× volume.

Egg Whites: Use 65-75°F whites. Large, deep bowl (not aluminum or plastic). Beat with thin wire whisk: begin slowly, increase tempo, beat until glossy, standing in peaks that are firm but soft and elastic (stiff but not dry). Inverted bowl test indicates possibly overbeaten (too dry). Approximately 300 strokes/2 minutes for 2 whites; 2½-4× volume increase.

Folding: Always manual. Add heavier mixture to lighter. Cut down through center, lift up and over, turn bowl, repeat. Work quickly but gently to retain air.

COOKING

Eggs begin to thicken at 144°F. High protein content requires moderate temperatures to prevent toughening.

Soft-Cooked: Hold at 185°F for 4 minutes (white tender, yolk thick cream)
Hard-Cooked: Hold at 185°F for 45 minutes (firm tender white, mealy yolk)

Poached: Slip into 185°F liquid; hold until white firm and yolk covered with thin white film. Liquid may be water, broth, milk, or vegetable liquor.

Baked: Combine with soft bread crumbs in buttered ramekins; bake in moderate oven (375°F).

COMBINING WITH HOT LIQUIDS

Always pour hot liquid onto beaten eggs slowly, stirring constantly. Condition eggs first by adding small amount of hot mixture to beaten eggs, then return to remaining hot mixture.

STORAGE

Do not wash until ready to use (protective coating on shell). Store in carton in refrigerator. Raw eggs and egg-containing foods (mayonnaise, custards) keep covered, refrigerated, away from strong-smelling foods.

Egg whites: Covered, refrigerated up to 4 days; then use only in cooked recipes
Egg yolks: Cover with water, refrigerated up to 4 days; or poach until firm and use within a few more days

Test for hard-cooked: Spin on pointed end—hard-cooked spins like top; raw topples over.
